content.full_text:
,State of California' 
Memorandum 
To 
Opinion Request Meeting Participants 
Date 
6-30-77 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ted Prim 
Sublect: Item for discussion. 
Several months ago, the Commission indicated that contributions 
should be "traced" from the donor through one or more intermedi-
ate entities to the ultimate recipient for the purpose of de-
termining where the donor should file campaign statements pur-
suant to Section 81005. 
The specific example before the Com-
mission involved a corporate major donor and its PAC. 
The 
Commission indicated that the corporation should file state-
ments as a committee supporting not only those candidates, 
measures or committees which it supported directly, but also 
in connection with all candidates, measures and committees sup-
ported by a PAC. 
Thus, if the corporation contributed to Propo-
s ition A in San Francisco and the PAC contributed to Candidate 
B in Fresno, the corporation would be required to file campaign 
statements in connection with both the San Francisco and the 
Fresno elections. 
In addition, the PAC would be required to 
file campaign statements in connection with the Fresno election 
but not in connection .with the San Francisco election. 
The Commission was not persuaded by the staff that this approach 
was too vague and that it unnecessarily complicated the already 
difficult process of filing statements pursuant to the Act. 
Ac-
cordingly, the staff was directed to develop standards for de-
termining when one committee must file campaign statements in 
connection with the candidates or measures supported by another 
committee. 
The purpose of this memo is to get ideas from the 
staff as to where and how guidelines should be drawn in this 
area. 
The most obvious need for tracing occurs with respect to the 
situation in which one recipient committee makes a contribution 
to another recipient committee who makes a contribution to a 
candidate. 
If the former committee is not required to file a 
campaign statement in connection with the election, donors may 
be able to contribute to candidates in the election without 
being disclosed. 
Clearly, we can require the former committee 
to file if donors are earmarking funds for the election regardless 
of whether they are filtered through intermediate entities. 
Since 


OR ~eeting Part 
6-30-77 
2 
pants 
the Commission indicated that an earmarking standard would 
not be satisfactory, the problem is where to draw the line 
once we go beyond earmarking. 
One possibility would be to require tracing only where the 
donor and donee committees are affiliated with each other, 
e.g., corporation and PAC or union and COPE. 
However, in these 
two examples the donor committees are major donors rather than 
recipient committees. 
Even though the Commission wanted trac-
ing in these situations, the need for tracing seems much more 
compelling when both the donor and donee committees are re-
cipient committees. 
Accordingly, I'm not sure that a standard 
which relies on "close affiliation" between the donor and donee 
committees is particularly effective. 
Unfortunately, one relatively easy solution to this problem 
may be beyond the Commission's authority. 
The complexity, 
vagueness and multiplicity of filings could be largely elimina-
ted if the donor committee could file monthly statements with 
the Secretary of State's office and the Clerks of San Francisco 
and Los Angeles. 
This procedure would afford up-to-date dis-
closure with a minimum of confusion to the filers. 
In devising a standard for tracing, we must take into account 
the present procedure for filing abbreviated statements. 
That 
procedure requires that certain types of committees file cam-
paign statements in connection with an election only if the 
committee contributes $250 or more to persons running in the 
election. 
If the committee contributes less than $250, it 
need not file in connection with the election. 
If a donor 
committee contributes to a donee committee which contributes 
to a candidate, how do we determine whether the donor commit-
tee has contributed $250 or more to the candidate? 
TP:jo

sections.question_synthetic: Under what circumstances should contributions be 'traced' through intermediate entities to determine filing obligations, and what standards should apply when one committee contributes to another committee that then contributes to a candidate?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC memorandum, not a formal advice letter response to an external request. No traditional Q/C sections present. Document discusses contribution tracing policy and filing requirements under Section 81005. The document appears to be seeking input rather than providing FPPC conclusions, making it a working document rather than a formal opinion letter.

embedding.summary: This is a 1977 FPPC internal memorandum from Ted Prim to Opinion Request Meeting participants requesting discussion and ideas on developing standards for determining when committees must file campaign statements in connection with candidates or measures supported by other committees, particularly when contributions flow through intermediate entities.
